findings.message: T005 removes three public `dry check-approved` flags, but the negative parse coverage only exercises `--threshold`. Please add rejection cases for `--db-path` and `--workspace-root` as well so the CLI contract for all removed flags is pinned by tests.

`FixpointResolve` is classified as display-only here, so `execute_track_with_telemetry()` will return early and skip telemetry for `sotp track fixpoint-resolve`. That contradicts the command contract in the briefing and should be removed from the display-only match.

P1: this new CLI entrypoint has no happy-path or error-path tests. The module only exercises clap parsing and `format_fixpoint_step`, so regressions in `execute_fixpoint_resolve` itself (mapping `FixpointResolveInput` into `CliApp::fixpoint_resolve`, stdout/stderr emission, and exit-code propagation) would be untested.

P1: the new public CLI entrypoint is not covered by any happy-path or error-path test. The module only exercises clap parsing and `format_fixpoint_step`, so regressions in `execute_fixpoint_resolve` itself (mapping `FixpointResolveInput` into `CliApp::fixpoint_resolve`, stdout/stderr emission, and exit-code propagation) would slip through.

P1, testing: the new public wrapper [`execute_fixpoint_resolve`]( /home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core-002/apps/cli/src/commands/track/fixpoint_resolve.rs:32 ) is only covered by error-path tests; the only wrapper tests start at [`apps/cli/src/commands/track/fixpoint_resolve.rs:175`]( /home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core-002/apps/cli/src/commands/track/fixpoint_resolve.rs:175 ). There is no test that exercises the success path and asserts `CommandOutcome::success` is printed and `ExitCode::SUCCESS` is returned, so a regression in the CLI forwarding layer would not be caught.

`TrackCommand::FixpointResolve` exposes `fixpoint_resolve::FixpointResolveArgs` from a crate-private module. With workspace `warnings = "deny"`, rustc will emit a `private_interfaces`/`private_in_public` warning for this public enum variant and fail the build. Move `FixpointResolveArgs` to a publicly reachable module (or re-export it from `mod.rs`) before exposing it in `TrackCommand`.

The new `TrackCommand::FixpointResolve` branch in `items_dir()` is not covered by the accessor test suite in this file. That accessor feeds telemetry path resolution in `main.rs`, so a regression here would silently anchor telemetry to the wrong track tree. Add a unit test asserting `TrackCommand::FixpointResolve(args).items_dir()` returns `args.items_dir`.

The unchanged Rules section now contradicts the new workflow: it says to always run `cargo make ci-rust` before re-running `sotp dry check-approved` and points to “step 3 above,” but Step 3 is now the zero-violation no-fix path that explicitly must skip `ci-rust`. This makes the agent policy ambiguous and can defeat the D1 fast path the change is meant to introduce. Update this rule to refer only to fix rounds / Step 4 verification.

Step 3 treats a `dry write` run with zero reported `Violation` findings as either approved or an anomaly, but Step 2 explicitly says already-judged pairs are cache hits/no-ops. In that state, a previously recorded unresolved violation will not be re-emitted by `dry write`; `check-approved` will still block, and this workflow tells dfl to stop as `blocked` instead of retrieving the stored violation/proposal via `bin/sotp dry results --filter violation` and fixing it. This makes a normal re-entry/retry with cached violations fail even though autonomous fixes are still available.

Step 4 can lose cached violations after a blocked follow-up gate check. `sotp dry write` returns no findings for already-verified pairs, including cached `Violation` records, so a non-zero `dry check-approved` after the follow-up write does not guarantee the “new finding set” is non-empty. Re-entering Step 4 using only that write output can spin/exhaust without applying available cached refactor proposals. The blocked branch should mirror Step 3 by reading unresolved cached violations before looping.

The diff changes `review-fix-lead` and `dry-fix-lead` from the existing Claude provider/model to Codex `gpt-5.5`, but the harness-policy briefing only scopes this file change to adding `dry-checker.fast_model`. These fixer-agent capability changes alter which external runner/model will be used for review and dry-fix loops, so they should be reverted or covered by the intended design and validation.

P1: `write_coverage` does not apply the repo's symlink rejection used by the other filesystem adapters. `atomic_write_file` only makes the rename atomic; it still follows the resolved `store_path`/parent path. If the coverage path or one of its parent components is a symlink, this adapter can read or overwrite a manifest outside the intended track directory. That is a filesystem-boundary regression compared with `FsDryCheckStore` and `FsCommitHashStore`.

P1: the new public adapter's tests do not cover the required deserialization error cases for malformed `path` and malformed `content_hash`. The implementation maps both to `DryCheckCycleError::CoveragePort`, but there is no regression test that exercises either branch, so a future change to domain validation or error mapping could silently break the contract described in the briefing.

`DryCheckConfig::load` stores `known_bad_injection_rate_percent` and `known_bad_detection_threshold_percent` as raw `u8` values without validating the `DryCheckPercent` invariant (`1..=100`). A v3 config with `0` will therefore load successfully and produce a `DryCheckConfig` that is already invalid for the usecase layer, so the loader is not fail-closed on malformed config. Validate these fields here or wrap them in the validated domain type before returning, and add an error-path test for the zero-value case.

`fingerprint()` rounds `threshold` to 6 decimal places before hashing. That makes the config fingerprint lossy: two distinct thresholds within the same 1e-6 bucket (for example `0.8500001` and `0.8500004`) produce the same manifest fingerprint, so `dry check-approved` can miss a real config change and reuse stale coverage.

`fingerprint()` hashes `threshold.value().to_bits()`, so `0.0` and `-0.0` produce different config fingerprints even though `SimilarityThreshold::new()` accepts both and downstream threshold comparisons treat them identically. A config rewritten with `-0.0` would falsely invalidate an otherwise unchanged coverage manifest and block `check-approved` unnecessarily.

This fallback turns any malformed `config_fingerprint` into the fail-closed sentinel, so a corrupted v2 record is accepted as stale history instead of surfacing `DryCheckReaderError::InvalidData`. Keep the serde default for missing v1 fields, but propagate `DryCheckConfigFingerprint::new` failures here.

[P1] [`/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core-002/libs/infrastructure/src/dry_check/store.rs:315`] A schema-v2 record that is missing `config_fingerprint` is silently deserialized to the all-zeros sentinel via the serde default path, so a corrupted on-disk record is treated as stale history instead of surfacing `InvalidData`. Keep the v1 migration default, but make missing `config_fingerprint` in v2 fail closed with an error.

Using `Option<String>` here, together with `skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none"`, breaks the v1->v2 migration on the first write. A legacy v1 file is read with `None` fingerprints, `append_record()` later rewrites the document with `schema_version: 2`, and the old records are still serialized without `config_fingerprint`. On the next `read_records()`, those historical rows are treated as schema-v2 records missing a required field and the store returns `InvalidData`, so appending to pre-upgrade history can brick the file.

P1: [`store.rs:346`](file:///home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core-002/libs/infrastructure/src/dry_check/store.rs#L346) turns any malformed persisted `config_fingerprint` into `InvalidData`, which aborts the entire `read_records()` call. The migration intent here is fail-closed, not fatal: a bad fingerprint should degrade to `DryCheckConfigFingerprint::fail_closed()` so one corrupt historical row does not make the full dry-check history unreadable.

P1: [`codec.rs:65`](file:///home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core-002/libs/infrastructure/src/dry_check/codec.rs#L65) and [`store.rs:225`](file:///home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core-002/libs/infrastructure/src/dry_check/store.rs#L225) keep `config_fingerprint` as `Option<String>` with `skip_serializing_if`, so a schema-v2 file missing the field is parsed as `None` and can be written back out without normalizing the fingerprint. That preserves a broken v2 record and lets later reads keep failing instead of repairing or rejecting the corrupted row.

`fragment_ref_of` is still `pub(crate)` here, and `mod.rs` does not re-export it. The briefing’s new approval contract says composition must compute current `FragmentRef`s using this promoted helper; keeping it private either breaks that public usecase API or forces composition to duplicate the path/hash construction logic, which risks the gate using different identity semantics than the write path.

`test_run_dry_check_writes_coverage_record_with_all_diff_fragment_refs` is not runnable as written: it runs `run_dry_check` with two diff fragments but only configures one `expect_embed()` and one `index.expect_search()`, and it also leaves an unused `expect_embed_batch()` in place even though the corpus is empty. Mockall will fail on the second diff fragment (and again on drop for the unused corpus expectation), so the coverage assertions never run.

`test_run_dry_check_with_empty_diff_writes_empty_coverage_record` and `test_run_dry_check_coverage_port_error_propagated` both set `expect_embed_batch()` even though `run_dry_check(vec![], ...)` never calls `embed_batch` for an empty corpus. Those expectations will remain unmet at drop, so both tests fail before asserting the new coverage behavior.

P1: `append_record` errors still abort the loop immediately, so a single writer failure stops the remaining pairs from being judged/persisted. That violates the T010/CN-03 requirement that the batch run to completion and then return the first error afterward. The early return at `append_record(...)` means later pairs can be skipped entirely.

P1: The scoped fan-out can deadlock if `agent.judge()` panics after a semaphore slot is acquired. The slot is only released after `judge()` and `send()`, so a panic skips the decrement/notify path and the spawning thread can block forever in `sem_cvar.wait()`, preventing `std::thread::scope` from unwinding cleanly.

The shared test-fixture refactor weakened the field-level assertions that used to guard `DryCheckRecord` round-trips: `assert_record_full_fields` now only checks that the hashes are 64 chars, and the `mod.rs` test no longer checks the exact rationale string. A broken `FragmentRef` hash derivation or a wrong persisted rationale could now pass these tests, so the helper no longer protects the record-field contract as strongly as before.

This shared helper is documented as supporting non-`Violation` records via `expected_proposal: None`, but it still unconditionally asserts `DryCheckVerdict::Violation`. As written, it cannot validate `Accepted` or `NotAViolation` records, so the consolidation leaves a coverage hole for two public verdict variants.

The new field-access test derives the expected hashes with `content_hash_of(...)`, the same helper used by `make_fragment_ref_for_tests` to build the actual record. That removes the independent oracle for hash derivation, so a regression in the hash input or algorithm would still pass. The same pattern appears in `results_interactor.rs`.

The inquiry phase still aborts the whole dry-check run on the first fragment-level failure: `fragment_ref_of`, `collect_above_threshold_candidates`, and `candidate_pair_keys_for_diff` all use `?` here instead of recording the error and continuing with the remaining diff fragments. That violates the intended aggregate-errors / no-premature-shutdown behavior and means one bad fragment can prevent later fragments from being judged.

The new parallel path still spawns one scoped OS thread per candidate pair. `max_parallelism` only limits how many are active at once, not how many threads are created, so a large diff can still exhaust thread resources and panic inside `std::thread::scope::spawn` instead of returning a `DryCheckCycleError`.

`run_dry_check` now returns immediately when `first_error` is set, which skips `write_coverage` for the entire run. That regresses partial-failure runs: fragments that were successfully processed in the inquiry phase never get recorded in the coverage manifest, so the approval gate will later treat them as stale/uncovered. Coverage should be written before returning the aggregated error, or written unconditionally after phase 2.

`assert_record_full_fields` does not actually require a refactor proposal when the expected verdict is `Violation`; if `expected_proposal` is `None`, the proposal check is silently skipped. That makes the shared record assertion incomplete and can let a broken `refactor_proposal` field slip through these tests without failing.

`write_coverage()` is called before the aggregated `first_error` is returned, so a coverage I/O failure will mask any earlier agent/index/entry/writer error from the same run. That breaks the stated CN-03 policy of returning the first collected error after all pairs complete; if one pair times out and coverage persistence also fails, the caller gets `CoveragePort` instead of the original pair error.

Repeated candidates emitted by `collect_above_threshold_candidates()` during the k-doubling loop are collapsed here with `or_insert`, so only the first occurrence of a pair is ever judged. If that first occurrence hits an agent/entry/write error, later duplicate occurrences that the old sequential path would still attempt are skipped, which can drop a recoverable finding and violates the aggregate-all-errors/no-premature-shutdown behavior.

P1: The new calibration fail-closed path returns here before the coverage manifest is written. That regresses the existing guarantee that `run_dry_check` persists coverage unconditionally after processing diff fragments, so a calibration failure leaves the current fragments uncovered and keeps `dry check-approved` blocked on later runs.

When both calibration tiers fail, this branch returns `Err("calibration failed")` after persisting coverage but before writing any production verdicts. `check_approved()` treats a diff as `Approved` when the current `FragmentRef`s are covered and there are no violation records touching them, so a failed run can still leave the current diff approvable instead of fail-closed.

The escalation test does not actually verify escalation. The mock used by this test ignores `DryCheckJudgeTier` and returns `Violation` for production calls regardless of whether the interactor calls Fast, Final, or never calls Final at all, so a regression that persisted the provisional Fast violation without re-judging it would still pass. T012 explicitly requires Violation/Err production results to be re-run with `Final`; add a tier-sensitive mock, for example Fast returns `Violation` and Final returns `NotAViolation`, then assert the persisted result comes from Final and both tiers were called.

The fast-calibration-failure test does not prove that production pairs are re-run with the Final tier after Fast calibration fails. Its production branch returns the same `NotAViolation` for every tier, so an implementation that incorrectly reused the discarded Fast provisional result would still write one production entry and pass this test. The briefing calls out that the failure path must re-run the same production set with `Final`; make this mock record or vary production results by tier and assert every production pair receives a Final judgment before writing.

P1: if `write_coverage()` fails on a calibration-failure run, the code still returns `calibration_error` first and drops the coverage error. That masks a failed fail-closed write, so the old coverage manifest can remain in place while the caller thinks only calibration failed. The coverage failure needs to take precedence or be combined with the calibration error.

P1: `known_bad_probe_pairs()` silently filters out any probe whose `CodeFragment::new(...)` construction fails. Because the returned vector size feeds directly into the calibration denominator, a malformed probe fixture would quietly weaken the barrier instead of failing closed. This helper should surface fixture-construction failure instead of dropping probes.

`interactor.rs` now has 703 non-test lines before the `#[cfg(test)]` block at line 704, exceeding the repository's 700-line production module maximum. The new calibration helpers pushed this already-large module over the enforced size limit; move one of the helper groups, such as known-bad calibration orchestration, into a sibling module to bring the production section back under the cap.

`mod.rs` declares the new `calibration` and `known_bad` modules, but both source files are currently untracked, so they are omitted from `git diff`. Applying this patch as-is leaves these module declarations pointing at missing files and the usecase crate will fail to compile. Add `libs/usecase/src/dry_check/calibration.rs` and `libs/usecase/src/dry_check/known_bad.rs` to the tracked change set.

P1: `FixpointResolveCommand.current_branch` is never consulted in `FixpointResolveInteractor::resolve` ([fixpoint_resolve.rs#L69](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core-002/libs/usecase/src/fixpoint_resolve.rs#L69), [fixpoint_resolve.rs#L265](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core-002/libs/usecase/src/fixpoint_resolve.rs#L265)). That leaves `FixpointResolveError::TrackNotActive` unreachable and removes the active-track guard expected for this workflow, so the resolver can drive DFP/RFP/ref-verify for a track while the user is on an unrelated branch.

`FixpointResolveInteractor::new` now requires a `TrackActiveBranchPort` and `resolve` performs an active-branch query before the three gate checks. The T014 usecase contract describes this resolver as a public-API composition of only the dry, review, and ref-verify gates, with the constructor wired from those three dependencies. This extra public port changes the DI surface and can force downstream composition to provide an uncatalogued dependency before the resolver can be used, so the fixpoint resolver API no longer matches the planned usecase boundary.

The verified-set seeding accepts any historical record with the current config fingerprint, not the latest record for that pair. If a pair was judged under config A, then later under config B, and the current config returns to A, this code will skip re-judging because the old A record matches. It then writes coverage with fingerprint A, while `check_approved` uses latest-per-pair history and can still act on the newer B verdict, allowing the dry gate to approve or block based on stale config. Seed from the latest record per pair, or force a new append whenever the latest record's fingerprint differs from the current one.

Skipping calibration when `work_items` is empty means fully cached runs no longer execute the known-bad probes. That removes the agent-quality check on cache-hit paths, so a regressed or broken agent can keep reusing stale verdicts and `dry write` will still return success until a new unverified pair shows up.

The new no-work calibration test does not actually assert that calibration probes were invoked. `make_probe_only_agent` only panics on non-probe production calls and, without a `.times(...)` expectation or counter, also passes if the agent is never called. A regression that skips calibration when `work_items` is empty would still return `Ok(vec![])` and write normal coverage, so this test would not catch the Round-7 behavior it claims to cover. Add an explicit probe-call count or tier/path assertion.

T003 is internally inconsistent about `DryCheckApprovalService::check_approved`: the task first says to change it to `check_approved(&self) -> Result<...>` and then immediately changes it again to `check_approved(&self, current_fragment_hashes: &BTreeSet<FragmentContentHash>) -> Result<...>`. That leaves the approval API underspecified and conflicts with the existing type catalogue, which still records the service as parameterless.

T014/T015 do not allocate the ports needed to satisfy the documented D2 behavior. The interactor docs say it must combine dry approval, review-state, and ref-verify result ports, but the constructor in the task injects `dry_approval`, `dry_checker`, and `coverage`, where `coverage` is a D5-specific port and the review/ref-verify inputs are missing. As written, the resolver cannot compute the required 3-gate state.
`T005` references `AN-12`, but this track only defines `AC-12` / `CN-09` anchors. As written, the regression-test requirement points at a nonexistent spec anchor and is a broken cross-reference.

`T012` / `T016` require `CodexDryChecker::new` to accept model or config inputs, but the infrastructure catalogue still models `CodexDryChecker::new` as parameterless. The planned API is inconsistent across the artefacts, so the checker cannot be wired to the new fast/final models as described.

`T008` adds a `max_parallelism()` accessor to `DryCheckConfig`, but the infrastructure catalogue only exposes `load()` and `threshold()`. The type catalogue and the task plan disagree on the public API needed by `T016`.

CN-04 says an omitted `max_parallelism` must fall back to a nonzero default, but T008 instructs implementers to make a schema_version=2 config missing `max_parallelism` fail with a Parse error. Those are mutually exclusive config semantics, so the task can be implemented in a way that violates the spec-level constraint.

The `DryCheckConfig` DTO catalogue drops the existing `threshold` field while marking the field list as complete (`has_stripped_fields: false`), even though T008 explicitly says the existing `threshold` is preserved and the same type still exposes a `threshold()` accessor. This leaves the infrastructure config contract internally inconsistent and would remove the similarity threshold from the planned schema.

The usecase type catalogue changes `DryCheckInteractor::new` to accept only `config`, while the implementation plan says the constructor keeps its existing embedding/index/reader dependencies and later adds coverage wiring into `DryCheckInteractor::new`. With only `config` in the planned constructor, the interactor cannot be wired with the ports required by T004/T010/T016, so the catalogue and plan are semantically inconsistent.

T012 says `DryCheckAgentPort::judge` should process all pairs together with known-bad probes as a batch, but CN-03 and T010 require preserving the `1 pair 1 agent_port.judge()` granularity and explicitly forbid packing multiple pairs into one agent call. This conflicts with the bounded fan-out design and makes the D3/D4 behavior ambiguous.

AC-07 requires the fast tier to mix known-bad probes into the batch, but CN-03 explicitly forbids putting multiple pairs into one agent call, and T012 then implements probes as separate one-pair calls. As written, the plan cannot satisfy both the acceptance criterion and the constraint; clarify whether probes are separate calibration calls or part of a batch.

CN-06 requires the known-bad probe injection rate to be configurable, and T011/T016 only add/pass the field. The actual two-tier implementation task T012 never says to use `known_bad_injection_rate_percent` to decide probe frequency or selection, so the implementation can ignore a required control while still completing the plan tasks.

AC-06 says wall-clock shortening must be confirmable via telemetry for runs with multiple unverified pairs, but T017 explicitly skips the wall-clock confirmation in CI and substitutes only a unit test that ReviewRound telemetry is emitted. That does not verify the acceptance criterion's performance claim, leaving AC-06 uncovered by the final validation task.

IN-04 requires adding both reasoning effort and probe settings to `.harness/config/dry-check.json`, but the D4 task only adds `known_bad_injection_rate_percent` and `known_bad_detection_threshold_percent`; the infrastructure config catalogue likewise lists no reasoning-effort field. As planned, T012 can only refer to an unspecified `reasoning_effort` setting, so the IN-04 requirement is not actually implementable from the plan artifacts.

IN-02 requires the fixpoint resolver to output the RFP target scope list, but the planned contract only models a single scope (`RunRfp { scope: String }`, `NeedsReview { scope: String }`, and CLI output `run-rfp scope=<scope>`). If more than one review scope is stale, the resolver cannot represent the required next work set, so the plan does not satisfy its own D2 contract.

`DryCheckCycleError::CoveragePort` is documented as covering coverage-record read/write failures, but the same variant is also assigned to `DryCheckParallelism::try_new` and `DryCheckPercent::try_new` validation failures. That collapses config validation errors into a coverage I/O error, making the error contract semantically wrong for callers and tests that need to distinguish invalid configuration from coverage-store failures.

Interface-breaking changes are split from their required composition rewiring, so the planned task order is not independently CI-passable. T010 changes `DryCheckInteractor::new` and T012 changes `CodexDryChecker::new`, but the `dry_write` call-site/config wiring for both is deferred to T016 after T008-T012 are complete. That leaves intermediate commits after T010/T012 unable to compile, and T013 is scheduled on top of that broken composition path. Move each required call-site update into the task that changes the interface, or insert an earlier wiring task before requiring CI between these steps.

T012 puts D4 calibration inside `DryCheckAgentPort::judge`, while also defining that call as judging exactly one production pair and then claiming a fast-tier calibration failure means all production pairs go to the final tier. That does not satisfy CN-07's all-pair retry guarantee: once `judge` is called per pair, a failure detected in one call cannot invalidate or re-run fast results already returned for other pairs unless there is a run-level/shared calibration mechanism. The plan needs to move calibration/failover to the batch/run level or specify a shared fail-closed coordination point before any fast result can be accepted.

AC-06 requires proving `sotp dry write` wall-clock improves versus a serial baseline using telemetry or measured wall-clock, and the implementation plan assigns that actual fixed-delay baseline/integration verification to T017. However `task-coverage.json` maps AC-06 only to T010/T013: T010 covers the parallel fan-out mechanics with unit tests, while T013 covers fast/final telemetry tagging. This leaves the artifact coverage map semantically inconsistent with the plan and can let the AC-06 performance verification be treated as covered before the only task that actually verifies it runs.

`FixpointResolveCommand` documents `current_branch` as having a non-empty invariant, but the catalogue models it as a raw `String` on a plain struct with no constructor or validation method. That leaves an explicitly illegal command state representable at the usecase API boundary, contrary to the plan briefing's type-design rule to make illegal states unrepresentable. Add a validated branch/current-branch value object or define a `try_new`/validation path for the command before exposing it to `FixpointResolveService::resolve`.

T004 directs the composition work through `dry_write`'s `DryCheckApprovalInteractor` construction and also updates the `dry_check_approved` path, but that is the check-approved interactor/path owned by T003/T005. T004's stated interface change is adding a coverage port to `DryCheckInteractor::run_dry_check`, so the plan is pointing implementers at the wrong composition seam for the dry-write wiring and can leave the actual dry-write constructor path unresolved.

The S5 section says T010's only prerequisite is T009, while the T010 task itself requires T008 because it loads infra `DryCheckConfig` and consumes the new `max_parallelism` DTO. Following the section-level dependency lets T010 start before T008 exists, so the task is not independently compile-passable in the advertised order.

T003's new approval algorithm scans `reader.read_records()` and blocks whenever any record involving a current fragment hash has verdict `Violation`, but dry-check history is append-only and the current semantics derive the latest verdict per pair with last-write-wins. If a pair has an older `Violation` followed by a later `Accepted`/`NotAViolation`, this plan would still block `dry check-approved`, contradicting the all-resolved requirement and potentially making the pure-read gate unable to approve resolved pairs. The task should derive latest-per-pair before evaluating verdicts.

IN-04 carries the D4 requirement to move dry-checker reasoning-effort configuration into `.harness/config/dry-check.json`, but the planned config/adapter surface only models `fast_reasoning_effort`: `CodexDryChecker::new` accepts `fast_reasoning_effort` plus `final_model`, with no final-tier reasoning-effort input. If implemented as planned, the final tier necessarily keeps an implicit or hard-coded effort, so the D4/tiered reasoning-effort configuration requirement is not actually implementable from these artifacts.

This in-scope telemetry requirement says the work stays within the existing telemetry schema, but then allows adding new fields or event types if needed. That conflicts with the same D4 telemetry plan in T013, which explicitly says to use existing `round_type` and add no new fields or event types, leaving implementers with two incompatible schema-change rules.

T008 defines `.harness/config/dry-check.json` schema_version 2 as the D3 schema with only `max_parallelism` added, while T011 later adds the D4 reasoning/probe fields but explicitly keeps the schema at 2. The final infrastructure catalogue models those new fields as required, so the same schema_version now describes two incompatible config shapes depending on task order. That makes the plan's config migration semantically inconsistent; either bump the schema again for the D4 fields or make the new fields explicitly defaulted/optional under schema_version 2.

T003 changes the public `DryCheckApprovalInteractor::new` constructor and `DryCheckApprovalService::check_approved` signature, but the composition call-site migration is explicitly deferred to T005 while the section also requires each task to be committed with `cargo make ci-rust` passing. That makes T003 physically unexecutable as an independent green task unless T003 also updates the composition call sites or preserves a compatibility path until T005.

`DryCheckCoverageRecord` and the new approval API track only `FragmentContentHash` values, so `check-approved` cannot prove that the current diff fragment itself was processed by the latest `dry write`. If a new or renamed diff fragment has the same content hash as a previously covered fragment at another path, the staleness check described in T003 will see the hash in `fragment_hashes` and can approve without forcing `dry write`, despite CN-08/AC-11 requiring fail-closed coverage for the current diff. The coverage key needs enough fragment identity to distinguish paths, not just content hashes.

`DryCheckCoveragePort`'s Signal changed from 🟡 to 🔵 even though this round is documented as a T001 progression marker only and `impl-plan.json` still marks T001, the task that introduces this port, as only `in_progress`. That makes the plan artifacts semantically inconsistent: one view now presents the port signal as resolved while the task state says the owning work has not completed. Keep this signal unchanged for the progression-only update, or move the task/artifacts together in a later completion transition.

T003 is marked done in the plan artifacts, but the generated usecase catalogue still reports the core T003 API changes as incomplete: `DryCheckApprovalService` remains Signal 🟡 here and `DryCheckApprovalInteractor` remains 🟡 at line 48. That contradicts the T003 completion claim and leaves downstream tasks depending on a supposedly completed pure-read approval gate while the catalogue still says the main service/interactor changes are not complete. Regenerate or update the plan/type artifacts so T003 completion and the type signals agree.

P1 contradiction: this round’s briefing says T009 deliberately did **not** add `Default` for `DryCheckConfig` and switched to `DryCheckConfig::new(...)`, but the plan artifacts still encode the old contract. `impl-plan.json` still says `DryCheckConfig` has a `Default` implementation, `plan.md` repeats that, and T010 still relies on `DryCheckConfig::default()`. As written, the plan is internally inconsistent and hands T010 an API the same round says should not exist. See [impl-plan.json](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core-002/track/items/dfp-rfp-loop-cost-reduction-2026-06-13/impl-plan.json#L53), [plan.md](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core-002/track/items/dfp-rfp-loop-cost-reduction-2026-06-13/plan.md#L46), and [plan.md](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core-002/track/items/dfp-rfp-loop-cost-reduction-2026-06-13/plan.md#L54).

P1 factual error: the regenerated catalogue view still marks `DryCheckParallelism` and `DryCheckPercent` as red, but the briefing says T009 is completed and these entries should be moving toward blue in the type-signal regeneration. That makes the artifact state misleading for the downstream plan gate. See [usecase-types.md](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core-002/track/items/dfp-rfp-loop-cost-reduction-2026-06-13/usecase-types.md#L16) and [usecase-types.md](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core-002/track/items/dfp-rfp-loop-cost-reduction-2026-06-13/usecase-types.md#L17).

The catalogue view regresses the two new `DryCheckParallelism`/`DryCheckPercent` entries from 🟡 to 🔴 even though T009 is marked done and the round briefing says these T009-owned types should move toward Blue. That makes the generated catalogue contradict the completion state instead of reflecting it.

T010 is marked `done`, but the task entry still has no `commit_hash`; the rendered plan likewise shows T010 checked off without the trailing commit hash shown for other completed tasks. This contradicts the briefing's transition contract that T010 should be completed with its post-commit hash populated, leaving the plan artifacts unable to trace the completed task to its implementation commit.

`plan.md` marks T012 as done, but this catalogue still leaves `DryCheckInteractor` at 🟡 even though T012 explicitly includes changing `DryCheckInteractor` to the calibrated two-stage flow. The plan artifact and the type catalogue disagree on whether the core interactor work is complete.

`usecase-types.md` marks the T012-mutated rows `DryCheckJudgeTier` and `DryCheckAgentPort` as already present (🔵), but the review briefing says the T012 commit has not been created yet and these type-signal rows should still be 🟡. That makes the generated type-signal artifact inconsistent with the stated plan state.

`T016` is marked `done` in the plan artifacts even though the briefing's design intent and checklist describe only completing `T015`. This also makes the rendered plan claim `16/17 resolved`, so the plan state now advances an additional task outside the described T015 update. Unless T016 is intentionally part of this review, this is a semantic plan-state contradiction and should remain `todo` in this change.

`DryCheckConfigFingerprint` is documented as controlling `check-approved` blocking when dry-check config changes, but the new catalogue entry has empty `spec_refs` and `informal_grounds`, which renders as a red Cat-Spec signal in the generated view. The briefing only accepts four yellow evaluator gaps, so this leaves a new behavioral domain type ungrounded in the plan artifacts.

The generated usecase type view now marks `DryCheckApprovalInteractor` as yellow, but the briefing's accepted deviations enumerate only four yellow type-signal gaps and do not include this interactor. That makes the plan-artifact signal set inconsistent with the stated accepted deviations for this round.

`DryCheckCoverageRecord` now requires a `config_fingerprint`, and the fingerprint is defined as a hash over all dry-check.json fields that affect write semantics, including `threshold` and tier reasoning-effort fields. However the `DryCheckInteractor::new` plan still only injects the existing usecase `DryCheckConfig`, whose catalogue fields are limited to the two known-bad percentages plus `max_parallelism`; no current config fingerprint is passed into the dry-write interactor. Since this same interactor owns `write_coverage`, the plan leaves no coherent source for the successful-run fingerprint it must put in the coverage record, making the new coverage schema unimplementable as specified.

The approval service contract still says `check_approved` decides using only `DryCheckCoveragePort + DryCheckReader` state, but this same catalogue now injects `current_config_fingerprint` into `DryCheckApprovalInteractor::new` and the domain coverage record contract requires blocking when stored and current config fingerprints differ. That is a semantic contradiction in the plan artifacts and can lead implementers/readers to omit the config-mismatch gate from the approval path.

The approval service catalogue now adds config-fingerprint mismatch as a staleness condition, but it still cites the D5/IN-05/AC-10 contract whose behavior is limited to coverage FragmentRef staleness plus all-resolved verdict checks. That leaves the plan asserting an extra blocking condition without behavioral-contract support, so readers cannot tell whether config changes are supposed to block `dry check-approved` or not.

`DryCheckApprovalService` now describes approval as checking only current FragmentRefs plus all-resolved history, but the same catalogue still says `DryCheckApprovalInteractor` checks both FragmentRefs and `config_fingerprint`, and `domain-types.json` says config mismatch must return Blocked. This public service contract is therefore inconsistent about whether config-change staleness is part of `check_approved`, which can mislead implementation or verification into approving stale coverage after dry-check config changes.

`DryCheckApprovalInteractor_new` is still modeled as depending only on `DryCheckConfigFingerprint`, but the updated usecase catalog says the constructor also takes `DryCheckReader` and `DryCheckCoveragePort`. The contract map therefore no longer matches the constructor contract shown in `usecase-types.json`.

`fail_closed()` is not actually guaranteed to force `Blocked` here: `DryCheckConfigFingerprint::new` accepts any 64-char lowercase hex string, so the all-zeros sentinel is a valid fingerprint and could compare equal to a legitimate current config fingerprint. The doc claim that this works "regardless of current config" is therefore not strictly true.

Both `DryCheckEntryError` and `DryCheckRecordError` are introduced in this round, but they are tagged `reference` instead of a change action. That makes the catalogue treat them as pre-existing dependencies rather than part of this round's API surface, which is semantically wrong for this plan artifact; the rendered markdown mirrors the same classification.

`T28_domain_domain_DryCheckRecord` and `T33_domain_domain_DryCheckRecordError` reuse node IDs that are already taken elsewhere in this same Mermaid graph (`T28_domain_domain_ReviewScopeSet`, `T33_domain_domain_ReviewScopeSetError`, and another `T33` in the usecase section). Mermaid treats node IDs globally, so these distinct types will merge in the rendered contract map and misrepresent the API surface.

This coverage manifest no longer includes the `libs/infrastructure/src/dry_check/store.rs` refactor called out in the round briefing, so `dry check-approved` will treat that fragment as stale and block the round even though it is part of the same update. The manifest is supposed to capture the full set of fragments processed by the latest `dry write`, so omitting a changed file is a semantic coverage error.

`dry_check_approved` now reads `track_dir/dry-check-coverage.json`, but this composition layer never writes that manifest on the `dry write` path. In this file the write flow still only constructs `FsDryCheckStore` and calls `run_dry_check`; `rg` shows no production `write_coverage` call. Since `FsDryCheckCoverageAdapter::read_coverage` returns `Ok(None)` for a missing file and the new approval interactor treats that as `Blocked`, a normal `sotp dry write` followed by `sotp dry check-approved` will fail closed forever unless a manifest is created out-of-band. Wire coverage persistence into the write flow or keep the approval bridge on an artifact that the write flow actually produces.

Removing `db_path`, `threshold`, and `workspace_root` from `DryCheckApprovedInput` makes the existing `execute_dry_check_approved` construction in `apps/cli/src/commands/dry.rs:197-200` no longer compile. The DTO change needs the caller updated in the same change set, otherwise the workspace is broken.

The new `GateEval` path in `dry_check_approved` is not covered by tests. There is no assertion for the Approved case emitting `gate_name="dry"` / `verdict="ok"`, and no Blocked-case assertion for `verdict="error"` plus the `blocked: N unresolved pair(s)` summary. This is public API behavior, so regressions here would be silent.

P1: The new GateEval path is only covered by the pure mapping helper tests near the bottom of the file. There is no test that `dry_check_approved()` actually reaches `emit_gate_eval(...)` after a finalized verdict, so a regression that removes or reorders the telemetry call would be silent because the emitter suppresses errors. Add an end-to-end test for both Approved and Blocked flows that asserts the telemetry write occurs.

`dry_tiered_telemetry_for_result()` derives `findings_count` from the final `dry_result` and reuses it for both tiers. On escalated runs, the fast ReviewRound event will report the final-tier finding count instead of the fast-tier count, so the new per-tier telemetry is wrong whenever the tiers disagree.

`dry_write()` passes the same `dry_start` instant to both `emit_review_round()` calls. That makes the final tier's duration include time spent in the fast tier, which defeats the per-tier timing this change is meant to capture.

P1: `dry_tiered_telemetry_for_result()` copies the same `verdict_parse_failed` flag into both tier telemetry objects for subprocess failures. When the final tier is the one that hits `IllegalOutput` on an escalated run, the fast tier's `ReviewRound` and `ExternalSubprocess` events are also marked as parse failures, which misattributes the error to the wrong tier.

`dry_write()` emits the fast-tier `ReviewRound` and `ExternalSubprocess` telemetry only after `run_dry_check()` has finished the entire cycle. On runs that escalate to Final, both fast events therefore measure through the final tier as well, so the new per-tier telemetry does not report an accurate fast duration.

`RecordingDryAgent` records every `judge()` call and increments `findings_count` for every `Violation`, but the dry-check interactor also calls the same port for known-bad calibration probes. Those probes are expected to return `Violation` even when there are no production candidates, so `dry_tiered_telemetry_for_result` will emit fast/final `ReviewRound` telemetry, and nonzero `findings_count`, based only on calibration activity. This violates the intended production tier telemetry semantics, including empty/no-candidate runs and final-tier emission when only calibration ran. The recorder needs to distinguish production judgments from calibration probes, or the telemetry helper needs to exclude probe calls from emission/counts.

The subprocess-error tier attribution uses `final_started_at.is_some() && !tiered.final_.has_completed()` to decide whether the final tier failed. `has_completed` is sticky after any successful final-tier call, so if one final judgment completes and a later final judgment returns `IllegalOutput`/subprocess failure, the final tier is incorrectly treated as not failing and `verdict_parse_failed` is assigned to the fast telemetry instead. Track the tier of the failing call directly, or record per-tier error state rather than inferring it from a sticky completion flag.

On successful runs, the fast ReviewRound is built from the final production `findings.len()` rather than the fast recorder count. In an escalated case where fast reports a violation but final clears it, the fast tier event is emitted with `findings_count = 0`, which violates the per-tier telemetry contract and underreports fast-tier findings. Use `tiered.fast.findings_count()` for the fast event and `tiered.final_.findings_count()` for the final event.

`findings_count` is now derived from `tiered.fast.findings_count()`, but `RecordingDryAgent` counts every `Violation` judgment, including the known-bad calibration probes that the interactor always runs. A clean dry run can therefore emit a fast `ReviewRound` with nonzero findings, and empty/no-production runs can still emit tier telemetry from probe activity. The previous success path used the actual `dry_result` findings, so this corrupts the per-tier telemetry counters with calibration-only verdicts.

The new tiered recorder/telemetry code pushes `dry.rs` to about 899 lines before the `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` block, well beyond the 700-line production module cap in AGENTS.md. This is a newly introduced architecture violation; split the tiered recorder/telemetry helpers into a production submodule to keep the composition module within the limit.

P1: The `Ok` path uses `findings.len()` for both tier payloads, so the fast `ReviewRound` reports the final accepted violation count rather than the fast tier's violation count. When Fast flags a pair and Final downgrades it to `NotAViolation`, telemetry emits `round_type="fast"` with `findings_count=0`, hiding the fast-tier escalation the design intent calls out as `fast_findings_count`. The fast count needs to come from production fast judgments, not the final result list.

P1: `tiered.fast.started_at()` is set by any fast `judge()` call, including calibration probes. `run_dry_check` still runs fast calibration probes even when there are no production work items, so an empty production diff can still emit fast `ReviewRound`/`ExternalSubprocess` telemetry here. That violates the requirement to skip tier telemetry when the production tier was never invoked and over-counts dry review rounds for calibration-only activity.

`is_calibration_probe` treats any repo-relative path whose first component is `probes` as a synthetic calibration fixture. Production fragments are normalized to repo-relative paths, so real code under a top-level `probes/` directory will be delegated but not recorded, causing `dry_tiered_telemetry_for_result` to suppress ReviewRound emission or undercount findings even though production `judge()` ran. Restrict this to the exact synthetic fixture identities or pass an explicit probe marker from the usecase layer.

On escalated runs the fast ReviewRound uses the precomputed fast-tier duration, but the following `emit_external_subprocess` call still passes the fast tier start instant. That helper computes `start.elapsed()` at emission time after final-tier work has completed, so the fast ExternalSubprocess duration includes final-tier processing and persistence time. This makes the per-tier telemetry internally inconsistent; use a duration-aware emission path or avoid emitting a separate fast ExternalSubprocess event with an elapsed-from-start timestamp.

The calibration-probe guard skips recording when either fragment has one of the synthetic probe paths. A legitimate production pair involving a repo file such as `probes/changed_a.rs` or `probes/candidate_a.rs` will therefore call `judge()` but never set the tier recorder's `started_at` or finding count, causing the required fast/final `ReviewRound` telemetry to be omitted or undercounted. The guard should identify the exact known-bad probe pair, not any single fragment path match.

P1: `CliApp::fixpoint_resolve` derives `project_root` only from the literal shape of `--items-dir` (`resolve_project_root(&input.items_dir)`), then uses that derived path to resolve `.commit_hash` and the diff base. Because it never verifies that `items_dir` is actually inside the discovered repo root, an absolute or nested path ending in `track/items` can steer the command at an unrelated tree and make the fixpoint result read the wrong gate state. This needs the same repo-containment check used by the other composition helpers.

P1: the new public entry point `CliApp::fixpoint_resolve` has no dedicated happy-path or error-path tests in this change set. The file only adds formatting/parsing coverage in `apps/cli`, so the branch guard, dry-base fallback, and gate-adapter error mapping are unexercised. That leaves a new public API without the required test coverage.

P1: `canonical_items_dir` is only checked with `starts_with(repo_root)` here, but the code never verifies that the path is actually a directory. A regular file named `track/items` inside the repo will pass validation, and the resolver will then treat `<file>/<track_id>` as a track tree instead of rejecting the input. This should mirror the `is_dir()` guard used by the other repo-root helpers.

P1: the new public API is only exercised for invalid inputs and the `run-dfp` path. There are no tests covering the successful `run-rfp`, `run-ref-verify`, or `commit` branches, so regressions in the new adapter wiring or `format_fixpoint_step` behavior on those paths would not be caught.

`build_current_fragment_refs()` still lets `GitDryCheckDiffGetter` discover the repository from the process CWD. `fixpoint_resolve()` validates `canonical_root`, but it never rebinds git operations to that root before calling `list_changed_hunks()`, so running the command from a different checkout or subdirectory can build `current_fragment_refs` for the wrong repo and return the wrong next step. Anchor the diff discovery to the validated repo root (or wrap the whole resolve in a repo-cwd guard).

`build_current_fragment_refs` temporarily changes the process working directory, but the tests in this file do not serialize on `crate::test_support::process_env_lock()` like the other env-mutating composition tests. `test_fixpoint_resolve_missing_coverage_record_returns_run_dfp` can therefore race with any concurrent test that also touches cwd and become flaky. Use the shared guarded pattern or lock these tests around the helper.

`build_current_fragment_refs()` normalizes fragments against `canonical_root` (the parent of `track/items`) instead of the repository root used by `GitDryCheckDiffGetter`. That works only when the track lives exactly at the repo root. If `--items-dir` points at a nested project inside the repo, the diff hunks stay repo-relative while the fragment refs become project-root-relative, so they stop matching and the dry gate can compute the wrong next step.

P1: `resolve_dry_diff_base_for_track` still depends on the ambient process CWD when it reads `.commit_hash` via `FsDryCheckCommitHashStore::read()`, but this command never anchors that read to the validated repo root. If `fixpoint_resolve` is invoked from outside the repository, the ancestry check can fail closed to `Ok(None)` and silently fall back to `git rev-parse main`, which can produce the wrong fixpoint step for a valid track. The new dry-base call site at [apps/cli-composition/src/track/fixpoint_resolve.rs]( /home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core-002/apps/cli-composition/src/track/fixpoint_resolve.rs#L391 ) should be wrapped with the same repo-CWD guard used elsewhere, or the store/read path should be made cwd-independent.

Relative `--items-dir` is rebased against `canonical_root` here, which is derived from the caller's current working directory, not the discovered repo root. That makes `fixpoint_resolve` resolve `track/items` to the wrong tree when the command is run from a nested subdirectory, unlike the other track commands that resolve paths through the git repo root.

Passing the raw `input.items_dir` into the gate adapters reintroduces CWD dependence after `canonical_items_dir` has already been resolved and validated. In particular, `FsRefVerifyGateStateAdapter` re-derives its project root from that path, so running `fixpoint_resolve` from a repo subdirectory can make ref-verify inspect the wrong track tree or fail even though the validated canonical path is correct. Use the validated canonical path here instead.

`fixpoint_resolve` validates and canonicalizes `--items-dir`, but then passes the raw `input.items_dir` into both `FsReviewGateStateAdapter` and `FsRefVerifyGateStateAdapter`. When the CLI is invoked with a relative `--items-dir` from any directory other than the repo root, those adapters re-resolve the path against the restored CWD instead of the already-validated repo-relative location, so review/ref-verify can fail or inspect the wrong track artifacts. Pass `canonical_items_dir` (or another absolute path) into both adapters.

`test_fs_ref_verify_gate_state_adapter_returns_approved_when_no_production_pairs` is tied to the current checkout branch (`track/dfp-rfp-loop-cost-reduction-2026-06-13`) instead of setting up its own repo/branch. That makes the test environment-dependent and brittle: it will fail on any run from a different branch or detached HEAD, which violates the test independence requirement.

P1: `test_fs_ref_verify_gate_state_adapter_returns_approved_when_no_production_pairs` is not independent: it assumes the workspace checkout is currently on `track/dfp-rfp-loop-cost-reduction-2026-06-13` and uses the live repo branch as an external precondition. On any other branch, or in detached-HEAD CI, `FsRefVerifyGateStateAdapter::ref_verify_status` will return the branch-mismatch error instead of `Ok(Approved)`, so the test becomes flaky. Build the fixture in a temp git repo with the expected branch, like `seed_track_repo`, rather than relying on host checkout state.

The coverage fingerprint is computed from `infra_config` after `--threshold` has already changed the effective write threshold, so a `dry write --threshold ...` records the same fingerprint as the file config. `dry check-approved` will then treat coverage produced under a different effective threshold as fresh, which can miss unresolved pairs.

This reads `.harness/config/dry-check.json` from `canonical_root` (the project root), but the dry-check writer and approval gate use the discovered repo root. In nested-project/monorepo layouts this loads the wrong config file or fails outright, so fixpoint resolution compares coverage against a fingerprint that does not match `dry write`.

`--items-dir` is no longer anchored to the caller's repo before repo discovery. For an absolute path that happens to live under some other git checkout, `discover_from(&canonical_root)` will switch the command onto that other repo and the later `starts_with(repo_root)` check will still pass, so `fixpoint_resolve` can return a step for the wrong repository instead of rejecting the input.

`fixpoint_resolve` now establishes the caller's CWD-discovered `repo_root` as the trust anchor, but the diff-base fallback still discovers git from `canonical_root` via `resolve_dry_diff_base_from_store(..., Some(canonical_root), ...)`. For a valid `--items-dir` inside the caller repo but under a nested checkout/project, a missing or malformed `.commit_hash` falls back to that nested repo's `main` SHA, then `build_current_fragment_refs` runs the diff from `repo_root`; this can make fixpoint resolution error or evaluate against the wrong base. Pass the CWD-discovered `repo_root` into the diff-base resolver so fallback and hunk discovery use the same repository.

`dry_write` now stores `infra_config.fingerprint()` even when `--threshold` changes the effective threshold. That drops the override from the coverage fingerprint, so a write run with a higher override than the file config can later be accepted by `dry check-approved` even though the manifest was built under a looser threshold and may be missing pairs in the current config range. This reintroduces the stale-coverage false-approval case that `fingerprint_with_threshold()` was designed to prevent.

The added test does not exercise `CliApp::dry_write` or the persisted manifest path; it only compares two `DryCheckConfig` fingerprint methods on the same config object. As written, it will pass even if the production code still stamps the wrong fingerprint, so it does not guard the regression.
